parent_chunk: My backyard garden has become a thriving oasis of vegetables and flowers. I planted tomatoes, cucumbers, and bell peppers in raised beds with rich, composted soil. Each morning, I water my plants and check for any signs of pests or diseases. The tomato vines have grown tall and are heavy with ripening fruit. I've learned that proper spacing between plants is essential for good air circulation and preventing mold. I also planted marigolds around the perimeter because they naturally repel certain insects. My herb garden includes basil, rosemary, and thyme, which I use fresh in my cooking. The satisfaction of harvesting vegetables that I've grown myself is unmatched. I've even started composting kitchen scraps to create nutrient-rich fertilizer for next season. Gardening has taught me patience and given me a deeper appreciation for where our food comes from

parent_chunk: One of my favorite culinary adventures has been learning to make authentic Italian pasta from scratch. I start by creating a well with flour on my kitchen counter, crack fresh eggs into the center, and slowly incorporate the flour until a dough forms. Kneading the pasta dough requires patience and strength, working it until it becomes smooth and elastic. After letting it rest, I roll it out thin and cut it into fettuccine ribbons. For the sauce, I simmer tomatoes with garlic, basil, and olive oil for hours until the flavors meld together perfectly. The aroma that fills my kitchen is absolutely heavenly. I've also experimented with making risotto, slowly adding warm broth to arborio rice while stirring constantly. Cooking has become my creative outlet, and there's nothing more satisfying than sharing a delicious homemade meal with friends and family

parent_chunk:
# Markdown Syntax
Markdown is a language for easily formatting your documents. This document can help you get started.

## Styled Text
To style text, wrap your text in the corresponding characters.
| Style         | Syntax              | Example           |
|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| emphasis      | `*emphasis*`        | *emphasis*        |
| strong        | `**strong**`        | **strong**        |
| strikethrough | `~~strikethrough~~` | ~~strikethrough~~ |
| underline     | `__underline__`     | __underline__     |
| code          | ``code``            | `code`            |
| spoiler       | `||spoiler||`       | ||spoiler||       |
| superscript   | `^superscript^`     | ^superscript^     |
| subscript     | `~subscript~`       | ~subscript~       |

## Links
To make text into a link, wrap it with `[` `]`, add a link destination to the end , and wrap the destination with `(` `)`. The link destination can be a web URL or a relative path to another Lockbook file.
```md
[Lockbook's website](https://lockbook.net)
```
> [Lockbook's website](https://lockbook.net)

## Images
To embed an image, add a `!` to the beginning of the link syntax.
```md
![Lockbook's favicon](https://lockbook.net/favicon/favicon-96x96.png)
```
> ![Lockbook's favicon](https://lockbook.net/favicon/favicon-96x96.png)

## Headings
To create a heading, add up to six `#`'s plus a space before your text. More `#`'s create a smaller heading.
```md
# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3
#### Heading 4
##### Heading 5
###### Heading 6
```
> # Heading 1
> ## Heading 2
> ### Heading 3
> #### Heading 4
> ##### Heading 5
> ###### Heading 6

You can also create a heading using an underline composed of `=`'s (Heading 1) or `-`'s (Heading 2) on the following line.
```md
Heading 1
===
Heading 2
---
```
> Heading 1
> ===
> Heading 2
> ---

## Lists
Create a list item by adding `- `, `+ `, or `* ` for a bulleted list, `1. ` for a numbered list, or `- [ ] `, `+ [ ] `, or `* [ ] ` for a task list at the start of the line.

> ===

## Lists
Create a list item by adding `- `, `+ `, or `* ` for a bulleted list, `1. ` for a numbered list, or `- [ ] `, `+ [ ] `, or `* [ ] ` for a task list at the start of the line.

## Lists
Create a list item by adding `- `, `+ `, or `* ` for a bulleted list, `1. ` for a numbered list, or `- [ ] `, `+ [ ] `, or `* [ ] ` for a task list at the start of the line. The added characters are called the *list marker*.
```md
* bulleted list item
- bulleted list item
+ bulleted list item

1. numbered list item
1. numbered list item
1. numbered list item

- [ ] task list item
- [x] task list item
```
>* bulleted list item
>- bulleted list item
>+ bulleted list item
>
>1. numbered list item
>1. numbered list item
>1. numbered list item
>
>- [ ] task list item
>- [x] task list item

List items can be nested. To nest an inner item in an outer one, the inner item's line must start with at least one space for each character in the outer item's list marker: usually 2 for bulleted lists, 3 for numbered lists, or 2 for tasks lists (the trailing `[ ] ` is excluded).
```md
* This is a bulleted list
    * An inner item needs at least 2 spaces
1. This is a numbered list
    1. An inner item needs at least 3 spaces
* [ ] This is a task list
    * [ ] An inner item needs at least 2 spaces
```
> * This is a bulleted list
>   * An inner item needs at least 2 spaces
> 1. This is a numbered list
>    1. An inner item needs at least 3 spaces
> * [ ] This is a task list
>   * [ ] An inner item needs at least 2 spaces

List items can contain formatted content. For non-text content, each line must start with the same number of spaces as an inner list item would.
```md
* This item contains text,
    > a quote
    ### and a heading.
* This item contains two lines of text.
The second line doesn't need spaces.
```
> * This item contains text,
>   > a quote
>   ### and a heading.
> * This item contains two lines of text.
> The second line doesn't need spaces.

## Quotes
To create a block quote, add `> ` to each line.
```md
> This is a quote
```
> This is a quote

Like list items, block quotes can contain formatted content.
```md
> This quote contains some text,
> ```rust
> // some code
> fn main() { prin

List items can be nested. To nest an inner item in an outer one, the inner item's line must start with at least one space for each character in the outer item's list marker: usually 2 for bulleted lists, 3 for numbered lists, or 2 for tasks lists (the trailing `[ ] ` is excluded).
```md
* This is a bulleted list
    * An inner item needs at least 2 spaces
1. This is a numbered list
    1. An inner item needs at least 3 spaces
* [ ] This is a task list
    * [ ] An inner item needs at least 2 spaces
```
> * This is a bulleted list
>   * An inner item needs at least 2 spaces
> 1. This is a numbered list
>    1. An inner item needs at least 3 spaces
> * [ ] This is a task list
>   * [ ] An inner item needs at least 2 spaces

List items can contain formatted content. For non-text content, each line must start with the same number of spaces as an inner list item would.
```md
* This item contains text,
    > a quote
    ### and a heading.
* This item contains two lines of text.
The second line doesn't need spaces.
```

## Alerts
To create an alert, add one of 5 tags to the first line of a quote: `[!NOTE]`, `[!TIP]`, `[!IMPORTANT]`, `[!WARNING]`, or `[!CAUTION]`. An alternate title can be added after the tag.
```md
> [!NOTE]
> This is a note.

> [!TIP]
> This is a tip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is important.

> [!WARNING]
> This is a warning.

> [!CAUTION] Caution!!!!!
> This is a caution.
```

parent_chunk: I've been following an intense fitness regimen for the past three months. Every morning, I start with a 30-minute cardio session on the treadmill, followed by weight training exercises. My routine includes bench presses, squats, and deadlifts to build muscle strength. I also incorporate dumbbells for bicep curls and shoulder presses. The gym has become my second home, and I can already see significant improvements in my endurance and muscle definition. I track my progress by monitoring the weights I lift and the number of repetitions I complete. Recovery is just as important as the workout itself, so I make sure to stretch properly and get enough protein in my diet. My fitness journey has taught me discipline and perseverance.

parent_chunk: Last summer, I visited the most beautiful coastal resort in Greece. The Mediterranean Sea was crystal clear with stunning turquoise waters that sparkled under the bright sunshine. I spent my days swimming in the ocean, feeling the cool waves wash over me. The sandy beach stretched for miles, and I loved walking barefoot along the shore, collecting seashells and watching seagulls soar overhead. In the evenings, I would sit by the waterfront restaurants and enjoy fresh seafood while watching the sunset paint the sky in shades of orange and pink. The sound of waves crashing against the rocks was incredibly soothing. I even tried my hand at surfing, though I spent more time falling into the water than actually riding the waves. It was the perfect getaway to relax and recharge

parent_chunk: Trees play a vital role in nature by producing oxygen, improving air quality, and providing habitats for many animals. Forests also help regulate the climate and protect soil from erosion.

parent_chunk: I've been working on a deep learning neural network project for image classification. The model architecture consists of multiple convolutional layers followed by pooling operations to extract features from input images. I'm using TensorFlow and Keras frameworks to build and train the network on a dataset of thousands of labeled photographs. The training process involves backpropagation to adjust the weights of neurons across hidden layers, minimizing the loss function through gradient descent optimization. I've implemented dropout regularization to prevent overfitting and used data augmentation techniques like rotation and flipping to expand my training set. The computational requirements are significant, so I'm leveraging GPU acceleration to speed up the matrix operations. After several epochs of training, my model has achieved 94% accuracy on the validation set. Understanding concepts like activation functions, batch normalization, and learning rate scheduling has been crucial to improving performanc

The training process involves backpropagation to adjust the weights of neurons across hidden layers, minimizing the loss function through gradient descent optimization.

The training process involves backpropagation to adjust the weights of neurons across hidden layers, minimizing the loss function through gradient descent optimization. I've implemented dropout regularization to prevent overfitting and used data augmentation techniques like rotation and flipping to expand my training set. The computational requirements are significant, so I'm leveraging GPU acceleration to speed up the matrix operations. After several epochs of training, my model has achieved 94% accuracy on the validation set. Understanding concepts like activation functions, batch normalization, and learning rate scheduling has been crucial to improving performanc
